intro_band.agent_remit_summary: The AutoGen code-executor family is the execution tier of a generator/executor pattern: `CodeExecutorAgent` extracts fenced Python and shell code blocks from paired-agent or group-chat messages and runs them, returning structured exit status and captured output. Execution is expected to happen inside an isolated environment — the Docker command-line executor is the recommended production path, with Jupyter, Azure dynamic-sessions, and a local-host executor as alternatives. The remit confines file I/O to a configured working directory, requires a wall-clock timeout on every execution, forbids the executor from initiating its own outbound messaging or network egress, and requires operator approval before using the local host executor in production, mounting extra host volumes, exposing GPUs, or raising resource ceilings.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Python library spanning `autogen-agentchat` (the `CodeExecutorAgent` in `_code_executor_agent.py`) and `autogen-ext` (the executor implementations under `code_executors/`). `CodeExecutorAgent` optionally drives an LLM `model_client` to generate, execute, reflect, and retry, and an optional `approval_func` gates each block. Executors share a common code-writing and `silence_pip` path in `_common.py`: `LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor` and `JupyterCodeExecutor` run on the host, `DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor` and `DockerJupyterCodeExecutor` run in containers, and `ACADynamicSessionsCodeExecutor` posts code to an Azure endpoint. `create_default_code_executor` prefers Docker but silently falls back to the local host executor when Docker is unavailable, working-directory confinement is enforced only for an optional `# filename:` header, and no executor emits a durable structured audit record of what it ran.

behavior_summary: The dominant pattern is isolation with the shape but not the substance of a guarantee: the container executors provide real host isolation, per-execution timeouts, and an approval hook, but the safe path is neither the default nor guaranteed. `create_default_code_executor` downgrades to unisolated host execution (`LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor`) on nothing more than a `warnings.warn` when Docker is absent, `approval_func` defaults to `None` so LLM-generated code auto-executes, and the local executor hands the executed code the parent process's full environment through `os.environ.copy()`. Compounding this, the Docker executor exposes no network-isolation or resource-limit controls by default and no executor writes a durable structured record of what it ran, so an untrusted code block can reach unapproved host execution with open network egress and no audit trail. The local executor's own docstring even claims a dangerous-command sanitizer that does not exist anywhere in the code.

rules.rule_text: Extract code from properly formatted markdown code blocks (triple-backtick fences) in incoming messages and execute only code in supported languages (Python and shell for command-line executors; Python only for the Jupyter and Azure executors).
Execute each code block inside the configured execution environment, in the order received, and return a structured result containing exit status and captured output.
Confine all file reads and writes to a configured working directory (for the Azure executor, `/mnt/data`).
Enforce a configured wall-clock timeout on every execution and terminate any process that exceeds it.
When configured with an approval function, obtain approval for each code block before executing it.
Surface all errors (syntax, runtime, timeout, out-of-memory) back to the caller in structured form without silently discarding them.
Sending email, SMS, webhooks, or any outbound message on its own behalf.
The executor must not initiate its own network traffic beyond a configured allow-list.

Any code source other than the paired agent's generated code blocks and code passed explicitly by operator application logic — code arriving from a file, HTTP endpoint, message queue, or memory store must not be accepted by the executor interface.
The paired AutoGen code-generator agent (the LLM component that produces code); code from this counterparty is the only accepted execution input.
Any mechanism that loads or executes code fetched from a remote URL or other unverified source on the LLM's behalf.
Any capability that persists execution results to long-term storage or carries state across unrelated sessions.
Reading or modifying the parent process's environment, credentials, or state from within an execution.
Moving files or data outside the configured working directory.
Executing code on the local host executor in a production deployment (default production deployments must use a containerized executor).
Enabling any network egress from the execution environment beyond a configured allow-list.
Mounting host volumes into a container executor at any path other than the configured working directory.
Any change to resource limits (CPU, memory, timeout) that raises the ceiling.
Exposing host GPUs or other host devices to a container executor.
Executing code with host-level privileges when a less-privileged option achieves the same task.
Loading or executing code from a remote URL or unverified source on the LLM's behalf; the paired agent's generated code is the only accepted input.
Silently swallowing or transforming error output before returning it to the caller.
Connecting to services, databases, or networks not explicitly permitted by configuration.
Actions that should never be retried: executions terminated for exceeding the timeout must be terminated, not re-run in a loop; approval-denied code must not be retried without new approval.
Every execution is recorded with timestamp, executor kind, language, source agent, working directory, timeout, exit status, and a digest (not the body) of the executed code.
The local host executor is used in a production deployment without operator approval.
A code execution attempts to read or write outside the working directory, or to escape the container/sandbox.

findings.summary: create_default_code_executor silently downgrades to unisolated host execution with only a warning, defeating the required container-approval gate.
findings.description: When Docker is unavailable, `create_default_code_executor` returns a `LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor` that runs LLM-generated code directly on the host, emitting only a `warnings.warn` rather than requiring operator approval or halting. The remit requires containerized execution by default and operator approval before the local host executor is used in production, and combined with the auto-approve default and the absence of any audit log, untrusted code reaches unapproved host execution with no gate and no record.
findings.policy_rule_text: Executing code on the local host executor in a production deployment (default production deployments must use a containerized executor). / Executing code with host-level privileges when a less-privileged option achieves the same task. / The local host executor is used in a production deployment without operator approval.

evidence.snippet: create_default_code_executor falls through to LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor with only a warnings.warn (lines 58-80) when _is_docker_available() is False — no approval, no error, no halt.
_execute_code_dont_check_setup runs each block as a host subprocess (cwd=work_dir) with no container isolation — the fallback target of the silent downgrade.

recommended_actions: In `create_default_code_executor`, do not silently fall back to `LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor`; raise unless the caller passes an explicit opt-in flag (e.g. `allow_local_fallback=True`) acknowledging unisolated host execution.
Route any local-host execution in a non-ephemeral deployment through the `approval_func` path so it cannot run without an approval response.

findings.summary: approval_func defaults to None, so all LLM-generated code is auto-executed with no human-in-the-loop gate.
findings.description: `CodeExecutorAgent.__init__` defaults `approval_func=None` and only issues a `UserWarning`; `execute_code_block` then skips the approval branch entirely and runs the code. The remit flags the no-approval default as a primary risk sensitivity and leaves mandatory approval an open operator decision, so untrusted generated code executes autonomously unless the operator wires in an approval function.

evidence.snippet: approval_func default None triggers only warnings.warn (lines 458-467); execute_code_block runs the approval check only `if self._approval_func is not None` at line 691, otherwise executes directly.

recommended_actions: Consider making `approval_func` required (or defaulting to a deny-by-default gate) for non-interactive deployments, rather than auto-approving on a bare warning.
Document and expose a supported "approval mandatory" configuration so operators can enforce human review without writing their own gate.

findings.summary: LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor docstring claims dangerous-command sanitization that is not implemented anywhere in the code.
findings.description: The class docstring states that command-line code is sanitized by regular-expression match against a list of dangerous commands to prevent self-destructive execution, but no such denylist or sanitizer exists — the only pre-processing is `silence_pip` and the optional `# filename:` workspace check. The phantom claim can mislead an operator into trusting the highest-risk executor as if it had a safety net it does not have.

evidence.snippet: Docstring claims "Command line code is sanitized using regular expression match against a list of dangerous commands"; a repo-wide grep for sanitize/dangerous/denylist returns only this comment line — no implementation.
_execute_code_dont_check_setup loop applies only silence_pip and get_file_name_from_content — no command sanitization before writing and executing the code.

recommended_actions: Either implement the documented dangerous-command sanitizer or remove the sanitization claim from the docstring so operators do not over-trust the local executor.

findings.summary: DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor creates containers with default networking and exposes no egress control, so executed code can reach the network.
findings.description: The container is created with no `network_mode` (e.g. `none`) and no allow-list mechanism, so it inherits Docker's default bridge network with outbound access. The remit forbids the executor initiating network traffic beyond a configured allow-list and requires approval for any egress, yet code running in the recommended production executor can open outbound connections and exfiltrate working-directory data by default.
findings.policy_rule_text: The executor must not initiate its own network traffic beyond a configured allow-list. / Enabling any network egress from the execution environment beyond a configured allow-list. / Connecting to services, databases, or networks not explicitly permitted by configuration.

evidence.snippet: client.containers.create (lines 537-550) sets volumes, working_dir, extra_hosts and device_requests but no network_mode — the container gets default bridge networking with open egress.

recommended_actions: Default the container to `network_mode="none"` and expose an explicit allow-list parameter so egress is opt-in per the remit.

findings.summary: No executor writes a durable, structured audit record of executions, leaving the remit's Log Only requirement unmet.
findings.description: The remit requires every execution to be recorded with timestamp, executor kind, language, source agent, working directory, timeout, exit status, and a code digest, but the components only make ad hoc Python `logging` debug/error calls and yield ephemeral `CodeExecutionEvent` objects into the message stream. There is no persistent, parseable action log, so executions cannot be reconstructed and escape or egress attempts cannot be detected after the fact.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every execution is recorded with timestamp, executor kind, language, source agent, working directory, timeout, exit status, and a digest (not the body) of the executed code.

evidence.snippet: Execution is surfaced only as a yielded CodeExecutionEvent (line 624) and the module's event_logger (line 47) is never called to persist an execution record.
_execute_command returns output/exit_code with no structured audit write; surrounding logging calls are debug/error diagnostics, not an action-level execution log.

recommended_actions: Emit a structured (JSON) execution record per block — executor kind, language, source, work_dir, timeout, exit status, and a SHA-256 digest of the code — to a durable logger the operator can route.

findings.summary: LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor passes the parent process's full environment to executed code via os.environ.copy().
findings.description: Before spawning each subprocess the local executor copies the entire parent environment into the child, so LLM-generated code inherits every host environment variable — including API keys, tokens, and secrets of the calling application. The remit explicitly forbids reading or accessing the parent process's environment or credentials from within an execution and classes those values as sensitive.
findings.policy_rule_text: Reading or modifying the parent process's environment, credentials, or state from within an execution.

evidence.snippet: env = os.environ.copy() then passed as env= to create_subprocess_exec (lines 397-434) — executed code receives all parent env vars including secrets.

recommended_actions: Build the subprocess environment from an explicit allow-list (PATH plus any operator-approved variables) instead of copying the full parent environment.

findings.summary: Docker executor sets no CPU or memory limits, bounding executions only by wall-clock timeout.
findings.description: `client.containers.create` is called with no `mem_limit`, `nano_cpus`, or `pids_limit`, so a single execution can consume all host memory and CPU until the timeout fires. The remit treats resource ceilings as approval-gated and calls out unbounded consumption as a risk sensitivity; only the wall-clock timeout is enforced.
findings.policy_rule_text: Any change to resource limits (CPU, memory, timeout) that raises the ceiling.

evidence.snippet: containers.create (lines 537-550) passes no mem_limit/nano_cpus/pids_limit; the only bound is the "timeout" command wrapper at line 360.

recommended_actions: Expose and default sensible `mem_limit`, CPU, and PID limits on the container, requiring operator approval to raise them per the remit.

findings.summary: The sources counterparty filter defaults to None, so code from any group-chat agent is executed rather than only the paired coder.
findings.description: In the non-`model_client` path, `extract_code_blocks_from_messages` runs `if self._sources is None or msg.source in self._sources`, so with the default `sources=None` every message source's fenced code is executed. The remit names the paired code-generator agent as the only accepted execution input, making the default a trust-expansion gap unless the operator sets `sources`.
findings.policy_rule_text: The paired AutoGen code-generator agent (the LLM component that produces code); code from this counterparty is the only accepted execution input.

evidence.snippet: extract_code_blocks_from_messages executes blocks when "self._sources is None or msg.source in self._sources" — default sources=None accepts code from any source.

recommended_actions: Document `sources` as a security-relevant control and consider requiring it (or defaulting to the paired agent) in multi-agent group chats.

findings.summary: extra_volumes and device_requests are accepted and applied with no gate or warning, allowing host mounts and GPU exposure beyond the working directory.
findings.description: The Docker executor constructor takes `extra_volumes` and `device_requests` and passes them straight into `containers.create`, mounting arbitrary host paths and exposing host devices with no approval prompt or restriction. The remit requires operator approval before mounting host volumes outside the working directory or exposing GPUs; the capability is operator-opt-in but the code provides no guard or acknowledgement step.
findings.policy_rule_text: Mounting host volumes into a container executor at any path other than the configured working directory. / Exposing host GPUs or other host devices to a container executor.

evidence.snippet: volumes={bind_dir: /workspace, **self._extra_volumes} and device_requests=self._device_requests passed to containers.create (lines 546-549) with no validation or approval.

recommended_actions: Log a warning (or require an explicit acknowledgement flag) when `extra_volumes` or `device_requests` extend the container beyond the working directory, so the approval requirement is visible.

findings.summary: Working-directory confinement is enforced only for the optional # filename: header; the code body is unconfined on host executors.
findings.description: `get_file_name_from_content` rejects filenames outside the workspace, but this only applies when a `# filename:` header is present — the executed code itself can use absolute paths, `..` traversal, or symlinks to read and write anywhere the host user can. On the host executors (`LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor`, `JupyterCodeExecutor`) there is no other confinement, so the remit's working-directory boundary holds only for the container executors that mount just `work_dir`.
findings.policy_rule_text: Confine all file reads and writes to a configured working directory (for the Azure executor, `/mnt/data`). / Moving files or data outside the configured working directory.

evidence.snippet: get_file_name_from_content only validates the workspace boundary for a leading "# filename:" line; code without that header is written and executed unchecked.
The subprocess runs with cwd=work_dir but no filesystem sandbox, so absolute-path or ../ file access in the code body escapes the working directory on the host.

recommended_actions: Document that host executors do not confine the code body and recommend a container/Jupyter-in-container executor whenever working-directory confinement is a requirement.

findings.summary: The default Docker execution image python:3-slim is a floating tag with no digest pin, exposing executions to base-image drift.
findings.description: `DockerCommandLineCodeExecutorConfig.image` and the constructor default to `"python:3-slim"`, a mutable tag that resolves to whatever the registry currently serves with no digest pin or integrity check. A compromised or shifted base image would change the execution environment for every default executor, a supply-chain rug-pull surface the remit's open questions flag as an operator decision.

evidence.snippet: image: str = "python:3-slim" (config line 71, constructor default line 160) — a floating tag pulled without a digest pin in start()'s images.pull.

recommended_actions: Pin the default image by digest (or document a pinned recommended image) and support an operator-supplied approved-image allow-list.

positives.title: Container executors provide genuine host isolation
positives.description: DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor and the Docker-Jupyter executor run code inside a container that mounts only the working directory to /workspace, giving real filesystem and process isolation on the recommended production path.

positives.title: Per-execution wall-clock timeout enforced across all executors
positives.description: Every executor enforces a configurable timeout and terminates the process/kernel/request that exceeds it, bounding runaway executions.

positives.title: Approval hook available and honored before execution
positives.description: When an approval_func is supplied, execute_code_block calls it with the code and context and returns an error result without running the code if approval is denied; the behavior is unit-tested.

positives.title: Errors surfaced, not swallowed
positives.description: Execution results carry the real exit code and captured stderr/stdout back to the caller, matching the remit requirement that errors never be silently discarded.

positives.title: Docker-Jupyter server generates a random auth token by default
positives.description: DockerJupyterServer defaults token to a 32-byte secrets.token_hex value and binds the published port to 127.0.0.1, so the kernel gateway is authenticated unless the operator explicitly disables the token.

positives.title: Core dependency pinned to an exact version
positives.description: autogen-ext pins autogen-core==0.7.5 exactly, reducing supply-chain drift on the primary runtime dependency.

rows.purpose: diagnostic messages for container lifecycle, pip install, and stop/cancel errors — not an execution audit trail
per-turn generation and execution events yielded to the caller UI; not persisted

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial (2.00). The executor family has genuine, operative Zero Trust primitives — real container isolation on the recommended path, a wall-clock timeout on every execution, an approval hook, and errors surfaced rather than swallowed — which keep it above Ad hoc. But the secure configuration is opt-in rather than default: silent host fallback, auto-approval, open container egress, a parent-environment leak, and unrestricted host mounts pull Zero Trust down to Partial, while the near-total absence of structured execution logging and of adversarial testing leaves Monitor Continuously and Build an AI Red Team at Ad hoc. Supply-chain management and domain scoping are the strongest areas, held back respectively by floating base-image tags and a default-open code-source filter.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The component is narrowly scoped to code execution — `CodeExecutorAgent` extracts only fenced blocks in `supported_languages` (default `python`/`sh`) via a compiled regex and the terminal description forbids other action — but the `sources` counterparty filter defaults to `None`, so in a group chat any agent's code is executed rather than only the paired coder's.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Untrusted LLM- or agent-generated code is the executor's primary input and is run with no validation of its source or content; there is no RAG/memory surface, but the default-open `sources` filter means code from any group-chat participant enters the execution path unchecked.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Real controls exist and run — container isolation on the Docker path, a per-execution timeout, an approval hook, and `# filename:` workspace confinement — but the defaults are permissive: `create_default_code_executor` silently falls back to host execution, `approval_func` defaults to auto-approve, the local executor leaks the parent environment via `os.environ.copy()`, and the Docker executor ships no network-isolation or resource-limit controls.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: The core dependency is pinned exactly (`autogen-core==0.7.5`) and the package is a mature, provenance-known monorepo, but executor extras are floor-pinned with `>=`/`~=` (`docker~=7.0`, `nbclient>=0.10.2`, `aiohttp>=3.11.16`) and the default Docker execution image `python:3-slim` is a floating tag with no digest pin.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: The test suites are correctness-focused — an approve/deny approval test and a single `# filename:` outside-workspace rejection test exist in `test_commandline_code_executor.py` and `test_code_executor_agent.py` — but there is no adversarial red-team program, sandbox-escape/injection corpus, or evidence that security testing drove architectural change.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: No executor emits a durable, structured, action-level record of executions (executor kind, language, source agent, exit status, code digest); only ad hoc Python `logging` debug/error calls and ephemeral yielded `CodeExecutionEvent` objects exist, so the remit's Log Only audit requirement is unmet and detection of escape/egress attempts is impossible.
